title: Side axe shaping
subtitle: Hewing a flat or tapered face onto green timber with a side-bevel axe
excerpt: The side axe is ground with a flat face on one side and a bevel on the other, which allows it to take a flat-faced cut along the grain of a green-wood billet without the wedging action of a symmetric axe. Used for squaring a log face, flattening a stool-seat slab, or roughing a drawknife-ready blank from round-section timber. This technique covers the stroke, the grip, reading the surface angle, and the grain rules that prevent splitting.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for side-axe hewing method. Beeler, The Tools That Built America (1976) for the American broad-axe tradition.

glossaryTerms.definition: An axe ground flat on one face and bevelled on the other, allowing it to hew a flat surface along the grain of a billet without the wedging action of a symmetric axe.
Shaping timber with an axe by taking a series of slicing cuts along the grain rather than splitting across it.
A short length of round or split timber, ready to be worked.

content.text: The
side axe
does one thing a symmetric carving axe cannot: it
hews

a flat, planed-quality surface along the grain of a round billet. The flat back of the blade rides against the work surface while the bevel of the edge lifts the chip; the result is a much flatter face than a symmetric-bevel axe can produce. It is used to square a round log face before drawknife or spokeshave work, to flatten one side of a green stool seat, or to reduce a sawn cant to a consistent thickness faster than a hand plane could.
What it does
A series of side-axe strokes along the grain of a
billet

produces a flat face in one tenth of the time a hand plane takes and without requiring the billet to be fully square first. The side axe is the preparation tool, not the finishing tool; a drawknife, spokeshave, or hand plane follows to remove the axe marks.
When to use it

When a round log needs a flat face before being worked further: squaring one face of a stool-seat billet before gouging the seat, flattening one side of a chair-leg blank before the drawknife taper, or producing a consistent face on a log for a workbench top. Not for fine shaping; that is the spokeshave and drawknife's territory.
What you need
A side axe with a sharp edge. A low side-axe block (a short log section at knee height for the billet to rest against during hewing). An India oilstone; a strop.
Safety

Stand clear of the line of the stroke at all times. The side axe is used in a chopping motion directed downward and away from the body; the feet must be behind the billet, not to the side where a miss would carry the axe into the lower leg. Keep the free hand well above the chopping zone.
Step-by-step
Step 1: Set up the billet

Lay the billet on the axe block with the face to be hewed upward. Mark a chalk line down the length of the billet at the target depth: how much timber is to be removed. A chalk line snapped across the end grain and along both sides gives the target plane.
Step 2: Score relief cuts

Before hewing, score across the billet at 5 cm intervals with the corner of the axe head, cutting down to the chalk line. These relief cuts break the grain into short sections so the side axe can remove each section without the cut running ahead of the target depth and splitting the wood along the grain further than intended.
Step 3: Hew to the line

Hold the side axe with the flat face away from the billet surface (facing outward). Swing the axe in a smooth, controlled arc that drives the edge along the grain, with the flat back of the blade tending to ride on the intended surface plane. Each stroke removes a chip from between two relief cuts. Work from one end of the billet to the other. The flat face of the side axe naturally limits how deep the cut goes; if it is going too deep, rotate the axe handle slightly to increase the flat-face contact with the surface.
Step 4: Read the grain at every pass

The side axe follows grain exactly like the drawknife: cuts that go uphill against the grain will split the billet surface rather than remove a clean chip. If a stroke produces a split that runs below the target line, the grain has reversed; hew from the other end of the billet instead. On most straight-grained billets one end is the right starting point; on complex grain, hew from both ends toward the middle.
What it looks like when it is right

A flat-hewed face will be clearly flatter than the original round surface, with small axe facets visible across the surface. The facets are the starting point for drawknife or spokeshave refinement; they are the expected result, not a failure.
Common mistakes
Skipping the relief cuts: the axe stroke runs along the grain beyond the intended section and splits the billet. Always score relief cuts before the main hewing passes.

Wrong face outward: if the bevel face rather than the flat face is held against the work, the axe acts like a wedge and drives into the wood rather than slicing along it. Check the axe orientation before each stroke.
